name: Lead Codices Jordan
description: The Lead Codices Jordan are a collection of around 70 metal (primarily lead) books or codices discovered in Jordan around 2008-2009. Proponents claim they are ancient Christian or Jewish texts dating back to the 1st century CE, possibly predating the New Testament Gospels. They believe the codices contain valuable insights into the earliest days of Christianity and potentially reveal previously unknown aspects of Jesus's life and teachings. The codices are said to be inscribed with images, symbols, and text in Paleo-Hebrew, Greek, and other ancient languages. Key evidence cited by proponents includes the style of the metalworking, the age estimations based on corrosion analysis, and the interpretations of the texts as potentially referring to early Christian beliefs and practices. However, mainstream scholars and archaeologists largely view the Lead Codices with skepticism. They cite concerns about the provenance of the codices (the circumstances of their discovery are unclear), the lack of rigorous scientific dating, and the interpretations of the texts, which are often considered speculative and lacking scholarly consensus. Many scholars believe the codices are modern forgeries created to deceive or profit from the antiquities market. The lack of archaeological context and the absence of independent verification have fueled doubts about their authenticity. The debate continues, with some researchers actively investigating their potential significance while others remain highly critical.
subtitle: Ancient texts or modern forgeries?

claims.claim: The codices date back to the 1st century CE.
claims.evidence: Corrosion analysis, style of metalworking, claims of ancient script.
claims.criticism: Lack of rigorous scientific dating, questionable provenance, stylistic inconsistencies.

claims.claim: The codices contain early Christian texts predating the Gospels.
claims.evidence: Interpretations of symbols and text as referring to Jesus and early Christian beliefs.
claims.criticism: Subjective interpretations, lack of scholarly consensus, alternative readings of the text.

claims.claim: The codices reveal previously unknown aspects of Jesus's life and teachings.
claims.evidence: Deciphered passages allegedly describing events not found in the Bible.
claims.criticism: Speculative translations, absence of corroborating evidence, potential for misinterpretation.

keyFigures.name: David Elkington
keyFigures.role: Promoter and researcher of the codices.

keyFigures.name: Philip Davies
keyFigures.role: Biblical scholar critical of the codices' authenticity.

timeline.event: Discovery of the Lead Codices in Jordan (allegedly).
Public announcement and initial media attention.
Ongoing debate and research regarding their authenticity.

sources.author: Davies, Philip R.
sources.work: Is This the World's Earliest Portrait of Jesus?
sources.title: Davies, Philip R.
